retrieved_docs.title: Dental Impression Materials -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Wax was the only impression material used in dentistry until the mid-19th century, when gutta-percha first appeared. Then, in 1857, Charles Stent created a thermoplastic modeling compound similar to today's impression compound. Still, the problem with this material was that it was rigid and could not reproduce undercut areas. All the impression materials used until that date became rigid after setting and could not copy the oral tissues accurately. Thus, there was always a need for an impression material that could remain elastic even after setting. That is when agar, a reversible hydrocolloid manufactured from algae, was introduced in dentistry. Although this jelly-like material was elastic, it required a complicated procedure to be used as an impression material.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Alginate Impressions -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Hydrocolloids were the first elastic materials used in the dental field. Hydrocolloids produce an imprint providing high-definition details despite undercuts. While the impressions deform upon removal, they later adapt to the original shape due to their elastic properties. Alginate is an elastic, irreversible hydrocolloid that offers lower costs, improved patient tolerance, ease of manipulation, reduced execution times, and the possibility of obtaining a detailed impression in a single step.

retrieved_docs.title: Viscoelastics -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Pseudo-plasticity
retrieved_docs.content: This is a unique property of viscoelastic. It refers to the ability of viscoelastic to change from gel to a liquid under pressure. It is also defined as the ability to pass from a state of high viscosity at rest to low viscosity at high shear rates. Sodium hyaluronate has greater pseudo-plasticity than hydroxypropyl methylcellulose (HPMC) and chondroitin sulfate. Sodium hyaluronate can be injected through a 30G needle, while HPMC will require a 25G needle. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Wound Dressings -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Hydrocolloids : There are cross-linked hydrophilic polymers with cellulose, gelatin, or pectin. Hydrocolloids can be in sheets, paste, or powder form; hydrofiber dressings are an available dressing variant. Advantages : They are initially impermeable to water but progressively absorb water, becoming more permeable and forming a gel. Hydrocolloids lower wound pH, inhibiting bacterial growth. These can be placed across joints or fill wound cavities. Disadvantages : It is not possible to visualize wounds. Clinical Applications : Use these for pressure wounds and minimal to moderate exudative wounds. Contraindications : These are not for necrotic or infected wounds. Frequency of Dressing Changes : Change dressings every 2 to 4 days. [2] [3] [16] [17] Alginate : This seaweed polysaccharide dressing has calcium ions exchanged for sodium ions to transform into a gel.

retrieved_docs.title: Emulsions -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: An emulsion is a mixture of two or more liquids that are usually immiscible but, under specific transforming processes, will adopt a macroscopic homogeneous aspect and a microscopic heterogeneous one. In an emulsion, one liquid is dispersed in the other. There are several types of emulsions:

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Alginate Impressions -- Technique or Treatment -- Disinfection of Impressions
retrieved_docs.content: The first step in disinfecting impressions is to rinse them well with tap water, which removes a significant number of microorganisms from the impression. [5] The most effective method for disinfecting an impression is by immersing it in a disinfectant liquid for 30 minutes. [14] This is more advantageous for hydrophobic materials because they only experience a slight distortion when disinfected this way. [14] By contrast, irreversible hydrocolloids are hydrophilic materials and undergo distortion when immersed in a solution.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Alginate Impressions -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Impression materials are classified as either elastic or non-elastic based on mechanical properties. [1] Elastic materials are capable of stretching, compressing, and recovering after deformation. Elastic materials comprise reversible and irreversible hydrocolloids, addition and condensation silicones, polysulfides, and polyether. [2] Nonelastic or rigid impression materials include impression compounds, zinc oxide eugenol, and impression waxes. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Complex Wound Management -- Technique or Treatment -- Dressing
retrieved_docs.content: Hydrocolloid: It is formed of the inner layer of self-adhesive gel-forming hydrophilic colloid particles such as carboxymethylcellulose (CMC). This layer can absorb exudate, allow for a moisture healing environment, and provide thermal insulation. The outer protective layer is formed of polyurethane which acts as a mechanical barrier for bacteria and foreign bodies. It is available in many forms, including paste, granules, and powder. It can be used in low to moderate exudating wounds such as minor burns and pressure ulcers. It should be avoided in clinically infected wounds. [43] [44]

retrieved_docs.title: Viscoelastics -- Preparation -- Chondroitin Sulphate and Sodium Hyaluronate
retrieved_docs.content: It is more viscous and has a better coating ability. [23]

retrieved_docs.title: Electrophoresis -- Introduction -- Factors Affecting the Electrophoretic Mobility of a Molecule
retrieved_docs.content: Fixed groups, such as sulfate, get ionized and acquire a negative charge at alkaline or neutral pH. When an electric field is applied, HO ions associated with these negatively charged groups start migrating toward the cathode. This movement hinders sample movement towards the anode and can reduce separation resolution. This phenomenon is known as electroendosmosis. To minimize its effects, ultrapure agarose gel with low sulfate content can be used. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Electrophoresis -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Tiselius used a liquid medium that had less resolution due to the effect of gravity and diffusion. Electrophoresis uses solid support media with buffers to overcome these obstacles. Molecules with similar charge, mass, shape, and size tend to move together and are separated into distinct bands or zones. Common solid support media include Whatman filter paper, agarose, cellulose acetate, and polyacrylamide. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Chromatography -- Introduction -- Column Chromatography
retrieved_docs.content: This is the most commonly used type, where a column of fiberglass or steel filled with silica particles acts as a stationary phase. [1] The mobile phase is gas or liquid. In normal-phase column chromatography, a polar stationary phase separates non-polar compounds; in reversed-phase chromatography, the nonpolar stationary phase and the polar mobile phase are used. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Emulsions -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Emulsions are a sub-class of colloids, which are two-phase systems of matter.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Epithelialization -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: In summary, wounds exposed to air and allowed to dry tend to heal slowly and result in poor cosmesis. On the other hand, moist wound environments improve wound healing, enhance angiogenesis, and reduce necrosis. [14] This knowledge has led to the production of various moist wound dressings, such as foams, hydrocolloids, alginates, and hydrogels. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Viscoelastics -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Viscoelastics are also known as ophthalmic viscosurgical devices. [1] They are solutions with dual properties. [2] They have viscous properties of a fluid and elastic properties of a gel or a solid. They have also been described to have viscous, elastic, and pseudoplastic properties. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Abrasion -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Abrasions are usually minor injuries that do not require extensive medical intervention. A sponsored study has shown that wet healing, using polyurethane and hydrocolloid plasters, is a more efficient and effective approach to wound healing. [19]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1838
retrieved_docs.content: Charcoal preparations that are used as intestinal absorbents are classified as members of the activated charcoal group.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1965
retrieved_docs.content: Corticosteroid dermatological preparations, plain encompass a range of topical corticosteroid strengths that are classified by potency groups. Within this category, “Corticosteroids, weak (group I)” represent the low‑potency end of the spectrum, while “Corticosteroids, very potent (group IV)” represent the high‑potency end. This hierarchical classification allows clinicians to select an appropriate plain corticosteroid preparation based on the severity of the skin condition, aiming to achieve therapeutic effectiveness while minimizing the risk of adverse effects associated with higher‑potency steroids.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 53
retrieved_docs.content: classifications, specific active‑ingredient combinations, dosing strengths, delivery methods, excipient roles, and critical drug‑interaction contraindications to optimize pain‑management regimens, maximize analgesic efficacy, and mitigate risks for individual patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 36
retrieved_docs.content: (hydrocortisone combinations, clobetasol). Recognizing these common excipients supports safe medication administration, informs compatibility assessments, and guides clinicians in managing patients with sensitivities to acidic or solvent components.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 1 -- Community 63
retrieved_docs.content: are classified as plain dermatological preparations, representing distinct potency subclasses of simple topical steroids. Conversely, “Corticosteroids, weak, other combinations”, “Corticosteroids, moderately potent, other combinations”, and “Corticosteroids, potent, other combinations” are subclasses of “corticosteroids, other combinations”, indicating formulation with additional agents rather than as stand‑alone preparations. The hierarchical organization of potency groups (weak, moderately potent, potent, very potent) and formulation types (plain vs. combination) clarifies how hydrokortison and related corticosteroids are positioned across therapeutic protocols, dermatologic products, and combination drug regimens, underscoring their central role in the management of inflammatory skin conditions and related disorders.

retrieved_docs.content: the pharmacologically active component is anhydrous dextrose (the active moiety), while dextrose monohydrate is listed as the active ingredient; together these anhydrous and monohydrate forms constitute the essential active substance of the formulation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 1 -- Community 83
retrieved_docs.content: (diclofenac 50 mg / misoprostol 0.2 mg and diclofenac 75 mg / misoprostol 0.2 mg) pair the NSAID diclofenac with misoprostol, which is placed in the therapeutic class Enzyme Replacements/Modifiers [TC] and linked to “other dermatological preparations” in ATC. A separate fixed‑dose product combines cholestyramine resin with diclofenac, linking an NSAID to an ion‑exchange resin derived from polystyrene polymers and associated with anion exchangers, antipruritic agents and hypolipidemic agents. Other hypolipidemic agents include colestipol, colestipol hydrochloride and loteprednol, the latter despite its usual classification as a corticosteroid. Hematologic agents are positioned as an inverse subclass of “medications and vaccines” and have hypolipidemics and protamines as child sub‑categories, reflecting overlap between blood‑related therapies, lipid‑lowering agents and protein‑based treatments. Enzyme‑based therapies for wound and ulcer treatment are grouped under “Enzymes for Treatment

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 1 -- Community 73
retrieved_docs.content: and systemic use. Plain dermatological corticosteroid preparations constitute a subset of dermatological products and can be formulated across the full potency spectrum—from weak (group I) through moderately potent (group II) to very potent (group IV)—allowing therapy to be tailored to skin disease severity. The taxonomy further defines “corticosteroids, weak/moderately potent/potent, other combinations” as subtypes of the general “corticosteroids, other combinations” class. Hydrocortisone sodium succinate is noted as a therapeutic option for patients in a vegetative state, reflecting its role in critical care.
